title: Employment Act 2002
chapters: 53 sections + 9 Schedules in 4 Parts. A major omnibus Act covering paternity/adoption leave and pay, maternity reforms, statutory dispute resolution, flexible working, written statement requirements, fixed-term employees, and union learning representatives. CRITICAL TEMPORAL NOTE: This Act is PARTIALLY SUPERSEDED. Part 3 (statutory dispute resolution — ss.29-33 + Schedules 2-4) was REPEALED by the Employment Act 2008 after the Gibbons Review concluded it was a legislative FAILURE that actually INCREASED rather than reduced tribunal claims. Parts 1-2 (family leave) remain in force but have been HEAVILY AMENDED. s.38 (written statement award) remains live and is being amended by ERA 2025.

parliamentaryOrigin.introducedBy: Government Bill — New Labour Government (second Blair ministry), Secretary of State for Trade and Industry Patricia Hewitt
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementDate: Various — phased from October 2002 to October 2004
parliamentaryOrigin.commencementNotes: Phased: fixed-term employee regulations (1 October 2002), paternity/adoption leave and pay, maternity reforms, flexible working, equal pay questionnaires, ULRs (6 April 2003), written statement reforms (1 October 2004), statutory dispute resolution procedures (1 October 2004 — subsequently REPEALED 6 April 2009).

significantAmendments.instrument: Employment Act 2008 — REPEAL of Part 3 (Statutory Dispute Resolution)
significantAmendments.effect: REPEALED ss.29-33, Schedules 2-4, and s.98A ERA 1996 (procedural unfairness). The Gibbons Review (March 2007) found the statutory procedures were 'an unhelpful atmosphere of legal formality', increased administrative burden, formalised disputes, and did NOT reduce tribunal claims. Replaced by the ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (in force 6 April 2009, revised 2015).

significantAmendments.instrument: Children and Families Act 2014 — flexible working extended to ALL employees
significantAmendments.effect: The ERA 2002 originally limited the right to request flexible working to parents of children under 6 (or disabled children under 18). The CFA 2014 extended it to ALL employees with 26 weeks' service. The ER(FW)A 2023 then made it a Day-One right.
From April 2024, paternity leave can be taken as 2 separate one-week blocks within 52 weeks of birth/placement (not just 2 consecutive weeks in the first 56 days).

significantAmendments.instrument: Employment Rights Act 2025 — Day-One paternity leave + s.38 amendment
significantAmendments.effect: From April 2026, paternity leave becomes a Day-One right (no 26-week qualifying period). ERA 2025 s.58(7) amends s.38(2)(b) and s.38(3)(b) — extending the written statement award provisions.

precedingDocuments.title: Work and Parents: Competitiveness and Choice (Green Paper, December 2000)
precedingDocuments.significance: The Government's consultation on proposals to help parents balance work and family, leading to the new paternity/adoption leave provisions and the right to request flexible working.

leadingCases.name: The Gibbons Review (not a court case — an independent review)
leadingCases.citation: Michael Gibbons, 'A Review of Employment Dispute Resolution in Great Britain', DTI, March 2007
leadingCases.principle: The statutory dispute resolution procedures introduced by the EA 2002 Part 3 were a legislative FAILURE. They created 'an unhelpful atmosphere of legal formality', imposed an unnecessarily high administrative burden, formalised disputes from an early stage, required external legal advice unnecessarily, created an expectation that disputes would end in tribunal, and were 'one size fits all' — hitting smaller employers hardest.
leadingCases.heldText: The Gibbons Review recommended repeal of the statutory procedures in their entirety and their replacement with a non-statutory ACAS Code of Practice. The Government accepted this recommendation.
leadingCases.significance: The Gibbons Review is one of the most significant policy documents in UK employment law. It led directly to the Employment Act 2008 and the repeal of the statutory procedures. Eileen must understand WHY the procedures failed — the same risks apply to any overly prescriptive procedural framework. The lesson: flexibility, proportionality, and early informal resolution produce better outcomes than rigid statutory steps.

parts.title: Part 1 — Provision-by-Provision Status: What's Live, What's Dead, What's Evolved

sections.title: The lifecycle of every EA 2002 provision — from enactment to current state
sections.summary: The EA 2002 is a partially superseded Act. Some provisions have been repealed, some have been superseded by later legislation, and some remain fully in force. Eileen must know the status of each provision to give accurate advice.
sections.text:
PROVISION-BY-PROVISION STATUS TRACKER (March 2026):

═══ PART 1 — PATERNITY AND ADOPTION LEAVE AND PAY (ss.1-16) ═══
Status: IN FORCE — but HEAVILY AMENDED

Paternity Leave (ss.1-4):
— ORIGINAL: 2 weeks' paid paternity leave, taken consecutively within 56 days of birth/placement. 26 weeks' continuous employment required.
— CURRENT (April 2024): 2 weeks' paternity leave, which can be taken as 2 SEPARATE one-week blocks within 52 WEEKS of birth/placement (Paternity Leave (Amendment) Regulations 2024).
— FROM APRIL 2026: Paternity leave becomes a DAY-ONE right — no 26-week qualifying period (ERA 2025).
— Statutory Paternity Pay: £194.32/week (from April 2026) or 90% of average weekly earnings, whichever is lower.

Adoption Leave (ss.5-8):
— ORIGINAL: 52 weeks' adoption leave (26 OAL + 26 AAL) with statutory adoption pay.
— CURRENT: Largely unchanged in structure. SAP rate matches SMP. Enhanced by shared parental leave provisions (CFA 2014).
— Key point: adoption leave applies to the PRIMARY adopter. The other partner takes paternity leave.

═══ PART 2 — MATERNITY LEAVE AND PAY (ss.17-21) ═══
Status: IN FORCE — framework remains but details in regulations

— ORIGINAL: Extended OML from 18 to 26 weeks. Reduced qualifying period for AML to match SMP (26 weeks' service by the 15th week before EWC). Extended SMP to 26 weeks.
— CURRENT: The maternity leave framework established by the EA 2002 is still the foundation: 52 weeks (26 OML + 26 AML), 39 weeks' SMP (6 weeks at 90% + 33 weeks at flat rate). Details in MAPLE Regulations 1999 (as amended).

═══ PART 3 — DISPUTE RESOLUTION (ss.29-33 + Schs.2-4) ═══
Status: ❌ REPEALED (Employment Act 2008, from 6 April 2009)

This is the provision that FAILED. The statutory 3-step procedures were:

Disciplinary/Dismissal Procedure (DDP):
Step 1: Employer writes to employee with grounds for action.
Step 2: Meeting between employer and employee.
Step 3: Appeal if employee is dissatisfied.

Grievance Procedure (GP):
Step 1: Employee writes to employer with complaint.
Step 2: Meeting between employer and employee.
Step 3: Appeal if employee is dissatisfied.

Modified procedures existed for gross misconduct (2-step DDP) and former employees (2-step GP in writing).

WHY THEY FAILED (Gibbons Review, March 2007):
(a) Created an 'unhelpful atmosphere of legal formality' — disputes were formalised from the outset.
(b) 'One size fits all' — the same rigid steps applied to a 5-person business and a 50,000-employee organisation.
(c) INCREASED tribunal claims rather than reducing them — the requirement to complete Step 1 before bringing a claim created a procedural hurdle that generated satellite litigation.
(d) s.98A ERA 1996 (inserted by EA 2002) provided that if the employer followed the statutory procedure, failures in other aspects of procedure would be disregarded if they 'would have made no difference' — this WEAKENED unfair dismissal protection.
(e) Employees had to raise a written grievance and wait 28 days before bringing a tribunal claim — this was criticised as a 'draconian penalty' that barred legitimate claims.

WHAT REPLACED THEM:
The ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (first issued 2009, revised 2015). The Code is NON-STATUTORY but has significant legal effect:
(a) Tribunals must take the Code into account when considering relevant cases.
(b) Unreasonable failure to follow the Code can result in an award adjustment of up to 25% (increase or decrease).
(c) The Code is 10 pages long (versus the complex statutory schedules) and sets out PRINCIPLES rather than rigid steps.
(d) The key principles: raise issues promptly, act consistently, investigate, inform the employee, allow representation, provide appeal.

LESSON FOR EILEEN: The failure of the statutory dispute resolution procedures is a cautionary tale. Rigid, prescriptive procedures imposed by statute can INCREASE rather than decrease conflict. The ACAS Code approach — principles-based, flexible, proportionate — produces better outcomes. Any employer designing internal procedures should follow the ACAS Code, NOT attempt to recreate the failed statutory model.

═══ SECTION 38 — WRITTEN STATEMENT AWARD ═══
Status: ✅ IN FORCE — being AMENDED by ERA 2025

s.38 provides that where an employee brings a SUCCESSFUL tribunal claim AND the employer is in breach of the duty to provide a compliant written statement (ERA 1996 s.1), the tribunal:
(a) MUST award a minimum of 2 weeks' pay, AND
(b) MAY award up to 4 weeks' pay if just and equitable.

This is NOT a standalone claim — it is an ADDITIONAL award on top of a successful substantive claim (unfair dismissal, discrimination, wage deduction, etc.).

ERA 2025 s.58(7): amends s.38(2)(b) and s.38(3)(b) — extending the circumstances in which the additional award applies. Monitor for the commencement order.

═══ FLEXIBLE WORKING — s.47 ═══
Status: ✅ IN FORCE — but SUPERSEDED in scope

The EA 2002 originally introduced the right to request flexible working for PARENTS of children under 6 (or disabled children under 18). This right has been PROGRESSIVELY EXTENDED:
— CFA 2014: extended to ALL employees with 26 weeks' service (from 30 June 2014).
— ER(FW)A 2023: made it a DAY-ONE right, 2 requests per year, 2-month response (from 6 April 2024).
The EA 2002 s.47 remains on the statute book but the substantive right is now governed by ERA 1996 ss.80F-80I as amended.

═══ FIXED-TERM EMPLOYEES — ss.45-46 ═══
Status: ✅ IN FORCE

Gave the Secretary of State power to make regulations implementing the EU Fixed-Term Work Directive (1999/70/EC). Implemented as the Fixed-Term Employees (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2002. Key provisions:
(a) Fixed-term employees must not be treated less favourably than comparable permanent employees.
(b) Successive fixed-term contracts exceeding 4 years result in the employee being treated as permanent (unless objectively justified).
(c) Waiver clauses (requiring employees to waive unfair dismissal rights at the end of a fixed-term contract) were OUTLAWED by ERA 1999.

═══ UNION LEARNING REPRESENTATIVES — ss.43-44 ═══
Status: ✅ IN FORCE

Inserted TULRCA 1992 ss.168A-168B, giving union learning representatives the right to paid time off to:
(a) Analyse learning and training needs.
(b) Provide information and advice about learning and training.
(c) Arrange and support learning and training.
(d) Promote the value of learning and training.
(e) Consult the employer about these activities.
(f) Undergo relevant training themselves.
The representative must be a member of a recognised trade union and have been certified by the union.

THE EVOLUTIONARY CHAIN — HOW UK DISPUTE RESOLUTION LAW PROGRESSED:

The EA 2002 dispute resolution provisions sit in a legislative chain that every employer and advisor must understand:

(1) PRE-2004 — POLKEY ERA: Unfair dismissal law governed by Polkey v AE Dayton Services [1988]. A dismissal could be unfair PURELY on procedural grounds. But compensation could be reduced to reflect the chance the employee would have been dismissed anyway (the 'Polkey reduction'). The ACAS Code of Practice (non-statutory) guided employers on fair procedures. This system was flexible, proportionate, and broadly effective.

(2) 2004-2009 — STATUTORY PROCEDURES ERA (EA 2002 Part 3): The statutory DDP/GP replaced the Polkey approach with rigid 3-step mandatory procedures. s.98A ERA 1996 provided that if the employer followed the statutory procedure, other procedural failures could be disregarded if they 'would have made no difference'. This WEAKENED unfair dismissal protection — the Polkey principle was partially reversed.

(3) 2009-PRESENT — RESTORED POLKEY ERA (EA 2008): The EA 2008 repealed Part 3 and s.98A, RESTORING the Polkey principle. The ACAS Code of Practice was revised and strengthened (25% award adjustment for unreasonable non-compliance). The law returned to: procedural fairness matters, but compensation can be adjusted to reflect the chance the outcome would have been the same.

(4) 2025+ — ERA 2025 ERA: The ERA 2025 builds on the restored Polkey/ACAS Code framework. It does NOT reintroduce statutory dispute resolution procedures. Instead, it strengthens individual rights (unfair dismissal qualifying period reduced to 6 months from January 2027, compensation cap removed), extends the written statement award (s.38 amendment), and creates new enforcement mechanisms through the Fair Work Agency. The lesson of the EA 2002 failure has been absorbed: principles-based guidance (the ACAS Code) works better than rigid statutory procedures.

This chain matters because it explains WHY the current framework works the way it does. An employer who asks 'why don't we have mandatory statutory procedures?' needs to understand that we DID — and they failed. The ACAS Code is not a gap in the law. It is the DELIBERATE REPLACEMENT for a system that made things worse.
sections.keyPrinciple: The EA 2002 is a study in legislative LIFECYCLE. Some provisions succeeded (paternity leave, adoption leave, ULRs) and remain the foundation of current rights. Others failed spectacularly (statutory dispute resolution) and were repealed within 5 years. Others were starting points that were progressively expanded (flexible working). Eileen must understand this lifecycle to give accurate, time-specific advice — and to explain to users WHY the current framework works the way it does.
refs: EA 2008 s.1 (repeal of statutory dispute resolution procedures)
Gibbons Review (March 2007) — review that led to repeal
ACAS Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)
ERA 2025 s.58(7) (amendment to s.38 written statement award)
CFA 2014 (flexible working extended to all employees)

regulatoryGuidance.title: Code of Practice on Disciplinary and Grievance Procedures (2015)
regulatoryGuidance.relevance: The ACAS Code REPLACED the failed statutory dispute resolution procedures. It is the governing framework for disciplinary and grievance handling. Failure to follow it can result in a 25% adjustment to tribunal awards.

commonErrors: Not knowing that the statutory dispute resolution procedures were REPEALED in 2009 — any employer or advisor still referencing the 3-step statutory DDP/GP is operating on legislation that has not existed for 17 years. The ACAS Code of Practice replaced them

Believing that failure to follow the ACAS Code automatically makes a dismissal unfair — it does not. The Code is not legally binding. But tribunals MUST take it into account, and unreasonable failure to follow it can result in a 25% adjustment to the award

Not updating paternity leave policies for the April 2024 changes — paternity leave can now be taken as 2 separate one-week blocks within 52 weeks, not just consecutively within 56 days. From April 2026, it becomes a Day-One right

Treating the EA 2002 as a dead Act — it is PARTIALLY superseded, but key provisions remain fully in force: s.38 (written statement award), paternity/adoption leave framework, ULR provisions, fixed-term employee protections

Not understanding s.38 (written statement award) — this is a live provision that catches employers who fail to provide a compliant s.1 statement. If the employee brings ANY successful tribunal claim AND the employer is in breach of s.1, the tribunal MUST add 2-4 weeks' pay. It is being amended by ERA 2025

Designing internal disciplinary procedures that recreate the failed statutory model — the Gibbons Review showed that rigid, prescriptive, formalistic procedures INCREASE conflict. The ACAS Code approach (principles-based, flexible, proportionate) is the model to follow

Not knowing that the right to request flexible working now extends to ALL employees as a Day-One right — the EA 2002 originally limited it to parents of young children. It has been progressively extended and is now universal. Employers with policies that still reference the parental qualification are out of date

versions.text: FULL FORCE: all provisions in force including Part 3 statutory dispute resolution procedures (from 1 October 2004). Flexible working limited to parents.
versions.amendedBy: EA 2002 as enacted and fully commenced
versions.policyRationale: The Blair Government sought to modernise employment law with both family-friendly rights and procedural reforms. The family-friendly provisions were popular and effective. The statutory dispute resolution procedures were widely criticised from the outset.

versions.text: PARTIALLY SUPERSEDED: Part 3 REPEALED (EA 2008). Flexible working scope expanded (CFA 2014, ER(FW)A 2023). Paternity leave amended (2024). s.38 amended by ERA 2025. Paternity/adoption framework, ULRs, fixed-term protections remain in force.
versions.amendedBy: EA 2008 (Part 3 repeal), CFA 2014 (flexible working), Paternity Leave Regs 2024, ERA 2025
versions.policyRationale: The Gibbons Review concluded that the statutory procedures failed to achieve their objectives. The ACAS Code replaced them with a principles-based approach. Family leave provisions have been progressively enhanced by successive Governments.

pendingAmendments.instrument: ERA 2025 s.58(7) — amendment to s.38 written statement award
pendingAmendments.status: ERA 2025 amends s.38(2)(b) and s.38(3)(b). Monitor for commencement order.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — extends the circumstances in which the additional award applies

pendingAmendments.status: From April 2026, paternity leave becomes a Day-One right. No 26-week qualifying period.
pendingAmendments.aceiImpact: MODERATE — affects all employers hiring new staff

surveillanceStatus.status: PARTIALLY SUPERSEDED — Part 3 repealed, remaining provisions amended
